1	# Artifact Generalization & Three-Layer Run Architecture
2	
3	Date: 2026-04-29
4	Status: Approved (refined through 3 review rounds with codex + fresh-eyes)
5	
6	## Summary
7	
8	Two threads converge in this plan:
9	
10	1. **Generalize artifacts beyond markdown documents.** Diagrams, wireframes, hero images, prompts — text and binary — become first-class artifacts via media-type plurality and sidecar `.ddx.yaml` files. Generated artifacts are checked into git; regeneration is source-hash-driven.
11	2. **Three-layer run architecture.** DDx owns three explicit layered primitives: `ddx run` (single agent invocation), `ddx try <bead>` (bead attempt in isolated worktree), `ddx work` (queue drain). The current two-class storage split (`.ddx/exec-runs/` vs `.ddx/executions/<attempt-id>/`) collapses into one substrate.
12	
13	These threads converge: **generating an artifact is a layer-1 agent run** with `produces_artifact` metadata.
14	
15	## Decisions (resolved through review)
16	
17	- **Artifact authority:** identity present → artifact. No "managed asset" second-class concept.
18	- **FEAT-019 fate:** child of FEAT-010 (not peer). Contracts to evaluation UX; workflow shapes (comparison, replay, benchmark) move to skills library.
19	- **Server posture:** 100% read coverage on HTTP/MCP for all DDx state, sequenced by impact. Writes case-by-case as workflows demand. Only `artifactRegenerate` write surface added in this plan.
20	- **Generated artifacts:** committed to git unconditionally. Size guard (50MB warn / 100MB hard error) with actionable git-lfs guidance; no auto-init / auto-`.gitattributes`.
21	- **CONTRACT-003:** amendments allowed if FEAT-006 work surfaces gaps. The mountable Cobra root export (below) is a load-bearing audit item.
22	- **Run substrate:** single on-disk shape; layer-2 and layer-3 add metadata. `.ddx/exec-runs/` and `.ddx/executions/<attempt-id>/` collapse.
23	- **No run-type catalog beyond the three layers.** Comparison, replay, benchmark, etc. are skill compositions, never enshrined in Go core or specs.
24	- **Loop ownership sharpened:** DDx owns mechanical queue drain (`ddx work`); content-aware supervisory decisions (e.g., "comparison failed → enqueue reconciliation beads") remain plugin/HELIX territory.
25	- **CLI verbs:** `ddx run` / `ddx try` / `ddx work` promoted to top-level. Hard-deprecate `ddx agent run`, `ddx agent execute-bead`, `ddx agent execute-loop` (exit non-zero with bare redirect message — no aliases, no grace period).
26	- **Generate-artifact layer placement:** pure-return generators (agent returns bytes/text, DDx writes file) are layer-1. Repo-editing generators are layer-2 with bead-less worktree.
27	- **Quorum dispatch:** moves to skill composition. `--quorum` flag goes away; replaced by `compare-prompts` skill.
28	- **`ddx agent` passthrough is structural:** DDx imports the upstream agent module's Cobra root and mounts it under `ddx agent`. Notionally `cli(ddx).agent(load_cli(agent))`. DDx does not enumerate per-subcommand wrappers; the entire upstream tree is the source of truth.
29	
30	## Three-layer architecture
31	
32	| Layer | CLI | Inputs | Outputs / side effects | Owns |
33	|---|---|---|---|---|
34	| 1 | `ddx run` | prompt + agent config | structured output, side effects, run metadata (tokens, model, duration, exit) | invocation atom; consumes upstream `ddx-agent` per CONTRACT-003 |
35	| 2 | `ddx try <bead>` | bead id, base revision | new worktree state + agent-run evidence; merge or preserve | worktree start/end capture; bead → prompt resolution; side-effect bundling |
36	| 3 | `ddx work` | bead queue, stop conditions | sequence of `ddx try` records + loop-level record (drained / blocked / deferred) | mechanical queue drain; no-progress detection |
37	
38	`ddx try` wraps `ddx run`. `ddx work` iterates `ddx try`. One on-disk substrate; layer metadata distinguishes records.
39	
40	`ddx artifact regenerate <id>` is sugar over layer 1 (or layer 2 if the generator edits the repo) with `produces_artifact: <id>` metadata.
41	
42	## Frame artifact updates
43	
44	### docs/helix/00-discover/product-vision.md
45	- Thesis: "documents AI agents consume" → "artifacts agents produce and consume."
46	- Artifact-management bullet: non-document media + generators.
47	- One sentence on producer/consumer space (no separate matrix).
48	- New Design-Philosophy subsection: **Three-layer run architecture** — `run` / `try` / `work` named as DDx's loop ownership.
49	- KVPs: "Multi-media artifact graph"; "Three-layer run architecture (run/try/work)"; note invocation is upstream.
50	- Principle #1: "Artifacts are the product (documents primary)."
51

107	## `ddx agent` subcommand fates
108	
109	`ddx agent` becomes a structural mount of the upstream agent CLI Cobra root. Per-file fate in DDx:
110	
111	**Delete with prejudice (upstream owns these via mounted subtree):**
112	- `agent_list.go`
113	- `agent_capabilities.go`
114	- `agent_doctor.go`
115	- `agent_check.go`

124	**Move to other DDx namespaces (DDx-only concerns):**
125	- `agent_executions.go` → `tries_*.go`
126	- `agent_log.go` → `runs_log.go`
127	- `agent_metrics.go` → `runs_metrics.go`
128	- `agent_usage.go` → folded into `runs_metrics.go`

131	**DDx-side overrides on the mounted subtree:**
132	- Deprecation handlers for `agent run` / `agent execute-bead` / `agent execute-loop`.
133	- `agent condense` (DDx-specific output filter — keep as override or move upstream during impl pass).
134	
135	**`agent_cmd.go` itself thins to:** mount upstream Cobra root + register DDx overrides.
136	
137	## Skill library (DDx-shipped workflows)
138	
139	Per FEAT-011's path model: source at `skills/ddx/`, embedded at `cli/internal/skills/ddx/`, init-time copies to `.claude/skills/ddx/`, `.agents/skills/ddx/`, `.ddx/skills/ddx/`.
140	
141	- `compare-prompts` — N-arm dispatch + aggregation (replaces `--quorum` flag and `agent benchmark`)
142	- `replay-bead` — re-run with altered conditions, baseline diff (replaces `agent replay`)

11	# Feature: DDx Agent Evaluation and Prompt Comparison
12	
13	**ID:** FEAT-019
14	**Status:** In Progress
15	**Priority:** P1
16	**Owner:** DDx Team
17	
18	## Overview
19	
20	DDx can dispatch the same prompt to multiple agent harnesses and capture
21	structured results. This feature adds the evaluation surface: sandboxed
22	comparison runs that capture side effects, automated grading of outputs,
23	and comparison records that make prompt quality measurable.
24	
25	This is deliberately an **optional evaluation and reporting layer** built on
26	top of the `ddx agent execute-bead` primitive (FEAT-006). The primary
27	bead-execution workflow is `execute-bead`, which runs an agent, evaluates
28	required executions, and lands or preserves the iteration. FEAT-019 provides:
29	compare, grade, replay, and benchmark capabilities over those preserved
30	iterations — it does not define a competing foundational execution model.
31	
32	### Ownership split
33	
34	- **FEAT-006** owns `execute-bead` (the canonical bead execution workflow),
35	  comparison dispatch (`ddx agent run --compare`), `ComparisonRecord`
36	  persistence, and arm execution mechanics.
37	- **FEAT-019** (this spec) owns **grading**, **replay**, and
38	  **benchmark suite execution** — the evaluation and reporting layer built
39	  on top of execute-bead preserved iterations and comparison dispatch.
40	- **FEAT-010** owns the generic exec substrate. Comparison records may
41	  use FEAT-010 storage if needed but comparison dispatch is not an exec
42	  projection — it has a fundamentally different shape (N parallel arms
43	  vs one sequential run).
44	- **FEAT-004** owns bead semantics including evidence. FEAT-019 adds
45	  evidence fields (session linkage) but does not modify bead close
46	  behavior.
47	
48	### Relationship to execute-bead
49	
50	`ddx agent execute-bead` is the primary primitive for agent-driven bead
51	execution. FEAT-019 replay and benchmark capabilities are built on preserved
52	`execute-bead` iterations: multiple `execute-bead --no-merge` attempts from
53	the same base produce a corpus that FEAT-019 can compare, grade, and report
54	on. "Try N ideas on this bead and pick the best" is a workflow plugin loop
55	that calls `execute-bead --no-merge` repeatedly and then uses FEAT-019
56	evaluation primitives — it is not a DDx execution mode.
57	
58	### Relationship to workflow tools
59	
60	DDx owns the comparison, grading, and replay primitives. Workflow plugins own
61	the policies that use them: when to experiment, which variables to sweep,
62	quality gates using experiment results, automatic model selection, and
63	exploration loops. "Try 10 ideas to improve metric X" is a workflow plugin loop
64	that calls DDx comparison primitives — it is not a DDx execution mode.
65	
66	## Problem Statement
67	
68	**Current situation:**
69	- `ddx agent run` can dispatch to any harness and capture output/tokens/cost.
70	- Quorum mode runs multiple harnesses and checks consensus (pass/fail).
71	- DDx Agent gives DDx full control of the agent loop with tool call logging.
72	- Beads define work. Commits capture verified results. But there is no
73	  structured way to compare harness outputs for the same prompt, replay a
74	  bead with a different model, or grade the quality of results.
75	
76	**Pain points:**
77	- When evaluating whether a local model (via DDx Agent) can replace a cloud
78	  model for a task class, there's no structured way to compare.
79	- Side-effecting agent runs (file writes, shell commands) can't be safely
80	  compared without isolation — running two agents in the same worktree
81	  produces interference.
82	- No way to answer "is this prompt good enough?" without manual review.
83	- Closed beads don't record which model/harness produced the result, so
84	  replaying with a different model requires manual prompt reconstruction.
85	
86	**Desired outcome:** Comparison, grading, and replay primitives that let
87	operators answer: "For this prompt, how does harness A compare to harness
88	B?" and "What if we reran this bead with a different model?" — with
89	concrete artifacts (diffs, outputs, grades) — automatically and repeatably.
90	
91	The canonical architecture is
92	[`SD-023`](../../02-design/solution-designs/SD-023-agent-evaluation.md).
93	
94	## Requirements
95	
96	### Functional
97	
98	**Sandboxed comparison dispatch**
99	1. `ddx agent run --compare --harnesses=agent,claude --prompt task.md` runs
100	   each harness arm in an isolated environment and records a comparison.
101	2. Each arm runs in a temporary git worktree created from the current HEAD.
102	   Existing `resolveWorktree` infrastructure is reused.
103	3. After each arm completes, capture `git diff HEAD` in the worktree as the
104	   "effect diff" — the concrete artifact of what the agent changed.
105	4. For DDx Agent arms, also capture the full `ToolCallLog` (every read, write,
106	   edit, bash call with inputs and outputs).
107	5. For subprocess arms (codex, claude, opencode), capture stdout/stderr and
108	   the effect diff. Tool call detail is not available.
109	6. Temporary worktrees are cleaned up after the comparison unless
110	   `--keep-sandbox` is specified.
111	7. Arms run in parallel by default (reuses quorum parallelism). Sequential
112	   mode available via `--sequential` for resource-constrained environments.
113	
114	**Side-effect capture**
115	8. The effect diff is captured as a unified diff string and stored in the
116	   comparison record alongside the text output.
117	9. For DDx Agent, the tool call log provides a complete audit trail: which files
118	   were read, what edits were made, what commands were run, what output they
119	   produced. This is richer than the diff alone.
120	10. Optionally run a test suite in each worktree after the agent completes
121	    (`--post-run "make test"`) and capture pass/fail as a quality signal.
122	
123	**Grading primitive**
124	11. `ddx agent grade --comparison <id>` sends a comparison record to a
125	    grading harness and records the evaluation.
126	12. `ddx agent grade --comparison <id> --grader claude` specifies which
127	    harness performs the grading (default: highest-preference available).
128	13. The grading prompt is a standardized template that presents:
129	    - The original task prompt
130	    - Each arm's text output
131	    - Each arm's effect diff
132	    - (Optional) test results
133	14. The grader returns a structured evaluation per arm:
134	    ```json
135	    {
136	      "arm": "agent",
137	      "score": 7,
138	      "max_score": 10,
139	      "pass": true,
140	      "rationale": "Correct implementation but missed edge case in..."
141	    }
142	    ```
143	15. Grades are appended to the comparison record in the session log.
144	16. Custom grading rubrics can be provided via `--rubric <file>` to
145	    replace the default template. DDx provides the rubric loading
146	    mechanism; the rubric content is user/workflow-defined.
147	
148	**Comparison reporting**
149	17. `ddx agent compare --list` shows recent comparison runs with summary.
150	18. `ddx agent compare --show <id>` displays the full comparison record
151	    including per-arm outputs, diffs, and grades.
152	19. `--format json` for machine-readable output (consumed by dun checks,
153	    workflow evaluation gates, CI pipelines).
154	
155	**Benchmark suites**
156	19a. `ddx agent benchmark --suite <path>` loads a JSON suite definition
157	     with arms (harness/model/tier configurations) and prompts, runs all
158	     prompts across all arms, and produces aggregate per-arm statistics.
159	19b. Suite format: `BenchmarkSuite` JSON with `name`, `version`, `arms[]`,
160	     `prompts[]`, optional `sandbox`, `post_run`, `timeout`.
161	19c. Output: per-arm summary (completed, failed, total tokens, cost,
162	     avg duration) plus full comparison records per prompt.
163	19d. `--output <path>` saves full results as JSON for historical tracking.
164	
165	**Bead-session linkage** (foundation for replay)
166	20. When a bead is closed after an agent run, record the `session_id` (from
167	    `sessions.jsonl`) and `closing_commit_sha` as bead evidence. The session
168	    already owns model, harness, tokens, and cost — do not duplicate those
169	    fields on the bead. `ddx bead show` resolves agent metadata from the
170	    linked session.
171	21. If no session link is recorded (manual close, legacy beads), replay
172	    still works but reports "baseline session unknown" and cannot provide
173	    a diff comparison.
174	
175	**Replay from bead**
176	22. `ddx agent replay <bead-id> --model <model> --harness <harness>` is the
177	    key primitive for answering "what if we reran this bead with a different
178	    model?"
179	23. Replay reconstructs the original prompt from the linked session (not from
180	    bead prose). If the session is unavailable, falls back to bead
181	    title/description/acceptance as a degraded prompt.
182	24. Replay base state: by default, replay checks out the `closing_commit_sha`
183	    parent (the state before the original implementation). `--at-head` mode
184	    replays against current HEAD instead ("would this model solve the task
185	    today?"). The default answers "would this model have produced the same
186	    result then?"
187	    For tracker-only backfills, do not point `closing_commit_sha` at a later
188	    metadata-only close commit; omit the field so replay falls back to the
189	    sessionless/manual baseline instead of pretending the metadata commit was
190	    the governed implementation diff.
191	25. Replay runs in a sandbox worktree, captures diff, runs `--post-run`
192	    evaluation, and reports a comparison of the new diff against the
193	    original commit's diff.
194	
195	### Non-Functional
196	
197	- **Isolation:** Worktree sandboxes must prevent cross-arm interference.
198	  Each arm sees a clean copy of HEAD.
199	- **Performance:** Parallel arm execution. Worktree creation is fast
200	  (git worktree add is <1s for typical repos).
201	- **Storage:** Comparison records include diffs and outputs which can be
202	  large. Use the same attachment-backed storage as session logs (FEAT-006).
203	- **Determinism:** For DDx Agent with the virtual provider, comparison runs
204	  are fully deterministic — enables CI-based prompt regression testing.
205	
206	## Design Principles
207	
208	### Platform primitive, not framework
209	
210	DDx provides the mechanics:
211	- Run N harnesses on the same prompt in sandboxes
212	- Capture outputs and side effects
213	- Send results to a grading harness
214	- Record everything
215	
216	DDx does **not** provide:
217	- Prompt optimization strategies
218	- Benchmark suites or leaderboards
219	- Model selection policies
220	- Grading rubric content (beyond a sensible default)
221	
222	Workflow tools compose these primitives. For example, a workflow plugin might
223	define: "Before promoting a bead, run the implementation prompt through
224	agent+claude, require grade ≥8/10 on both arms." A check runner might define:
225	"regression-test this prompt against the recorded baseline."
226	
227	### Sandboxing strategy
228	
229	Git worktrees are the natural sandbox for code-generating agents:
230	- Fast to create (copy-on-write on modern filesystems)
231	- Full git state (HEAD, index, config, hooks)
232	- Clean isolation (separate working directory)
233	- Cheap to clean up (`git worktree remove`)
234	- Diff capture is trivial (`git diff HEAD`)
235	
236	For DDx Agent specifically, the tool sandbox is already built into the
237	`WorkDir` parameter — setting it to the worktree path is sufficient.
238	For subprocess harnesses, `WorkDirFlag` (codex: `-C`, opencode: `--dir`)
239	achieves the same.
240	
241	No container or VM sandboxing is needed for comparison runs because:
242	- The threat model is "prevent cross-arm interference," not "prevent
243	  malicious code execution"
244	- Worktrees provide filesystem isolation
245	- Network isolation is out of scope (agents need API access)
246	
247	If stronger isolation is needed (e.g., untrusted model output running
248	shell commands), that's a future concern addressed by DDx Agent's tool
249	permission layer or external sandbox tooling.
250	
251	## CLI Interface
252	
253	```bash
254	# Compare two harnesses on the same prompt
255	ddx agent run --compare --harnesses=agent,claude --prompt task.md
256	
257	# Compare with per-arm model selection
258	ddx agent run --compare \
259	  --arm agent:qwen3.5-27b:fast \
260	  --arm claude:claude-opus-4-6:smart \
261	  --prompt task.md --sandbox
262	
263	# Compare with post-run test
264	ddx agent run --compare --harnesses=agent,claude --prompt task.md \
265	  --post-run "cd cli && make test"
266	
267	# Grade a comparison using claude as grader
268	ddx agent grade --comparison cmp-abc123 --grader claude
269	
270	# Grade with custom rubric
271	ddx agent grade --comparison cmp-abc123 --rubric rubrics/code-quality.md
272	
273	# List recent comparisons
274	ddx agent compare --list
275	
276	# Show comparison detail
277	ddx agent compare --show cmp-abc123 --format json
278	
279	# Replay a closed bead with a different model
280	ddx agent replay ddx-52d42ccb --model qwen3.5-27b --harness agent
281	
282	# Replay with post-run verification
283	ddx agent replay ddx-52d42ccb --model qwen3.5-27b --harness agent \
284	  --post-run "cd cli && make test"
285	
286	# Run a benchmark suite
287	ddx agent benchmark --suite benchmarks/implementation.json --output results.json
288	```
289	
290	### Configuration
291	
292	```yaml
293	# .ddx/config.yaml
294	agent:
295	  compare:
296	    default_grader: claude         # harness to use for grading
297	    keep_sandbox: false            # preserve worktrees after comparison
298	    parallel: true                 # run arms in parallel
299	    post_run: ""                   # command to run after each arm
300	```
301	
302	## User Stories
303	
304	### US-190: Developer Compares Local vs Cloud Model
305	**As a** developer evaluating whether a local model can handle a task class
306	**I want** to run the same prompt through DDx Agent (local) and claude (cloud)
307	**So that** I can see concrete differences in output quality and cost
308	
309	**Acceptance Criteria:**
310	- Given I run `ddx agent run --compare --harnesses=agent,claude --prompt task.md`,
311	  then both harnesses receive the same prompt text
312	- Given both arms complete, then I see a comparison summary with per-arm
313	  output, tokens, cost, and duration
314	- Given I add `--keep-sandbox`, then I can inspect the worktree contents
315	  after the run
316	
317	### US-191: Developer Grades Agent Outputs
318	**As a** developer assessing prompt quality
319	**I want** to have a smart model grade the outputs of a comparison run
320	**So that** I get a structured quality score without manual review
321	
322	**Acceptance Criteria:**
323	- Given a completed comparison, when I run `ddx agent grade --comparison <id>`,
324	  then a grading harness evaluates each arm
325	- Given the grading completes, then each arm has a score, pass/fail, and
326	  rationale in the comparison record
327	- Given I specify `--rubric custom.md`, then the custom rubric replaces
328	  the default grading template
329	
330	### US-192: CI Pipeline Runs Prompt Regression Test
331	**As a** CI system ensuring prompt quality
332	**I want** to compare agent outputs against a recorded baseline
333	**So that** prompt changes that degrade quality are caught before merge
334	
335	**Acceptance Criteria:**
336	- Given a comparison with `--harnesses=agent` and the virtual provider
337	  baseline, when the DDx Agent output diverges significantly, then the
338	  comparison record reflects the difference
339	- Given `--format json` output, then CI can parse the comparison and
340	  fail the pipeline on grade regression
341	
342	### US-193: Developer Captures Side Effects Safely
343	**As a** developer running comparison experiments
344	**I want** each agent arm to run in an isolated worktree
345	**So that** file changes from one arm don't contaminate the other or my
346	working tree
347	
348	**Acceptance Criteria:**
349	- Given I run a comparison with two harnesses that both edit files, then
350	  each arm's changes are captured as separate diffs
351	- Given the comparison completes, then my original working tree is
352	  unchanged
353	- Given an arm fails mid-run, then its worktree is still cleaned up
354	  (unless `--keep-sandbox`)
355	
356	### US-194: Developer Replays a Bead With a Different Model
357	**As a** developer evaluating local model capability
358	**I want** to rerun a previously-closed bead with a different model
359	**So that** I can compare the new model's output against the known-good result
360	
361	**Acceptance Criteria:**
362	- Given a closed bead with a linked session, when I run
363	  `ddx agent replay <bead-id> --model qwen3.5-27b --harness agent`,
364	  then the original prompt is reconstructed from the session and dispatched
365	- Given the replay completes in a sandbox worktree checked out at the
366	  parent of `closing_commit_sha`, then I see the diff compared against
367	  the original commit's diff
368	- Given `--at-head`, the replay runs against current HEAD instead
369	- Given a bead without a linked session, then replay falls back to bead
370	  prose and reports "baseline session unknown"
371	
372	### US-195: Bead Links to Agent Session on Close
373	**As a** developer building a replay corpus
374	**I want** bead close to record the session ID and closing commit
375	**So that** future replays can reconstruct the exact prompt and baseline
376	
377	**Acceptance Criteria:**
378	- Given an agent run closes bead ddx-xxx, then `session_id` and
379	  `closing_commit_sha` are recorded as bead evidence
380	- Given `ddx bead show ddx-xxx`, then model/harness/tokens/cost are
381	  resolved from the linked session (not stored on the bead)
382	- Given a bead closed without an agent run, then no session link exists
383	  and `ddx bead show` omits agent metadata
384	
385	### US-196: Evaluation Primitives Consume Preserved Execute-bead Iterations
386	**As** a developer evaluating bead execution quality
387	**I want** FEAT-019 evaluation tools to operate on preserved execute-bead iterations
388	**So that** comparison, grading, and benchmarking build on execute-bead results rather than a competing execution model
389	
390	**Acceptance Criteria:**
391	- Given multiple `ddx agent execute-bead <id> --no-merge` runs from the same base revision exist, when `ddx agent compare` or `ddx agent benchmark` consumes them, then those preserved iterations are valid inputs — no separate evaluation-specific execution path is required to produce the corpus.
392	- Given an evaluation consumes a preserved execute-bead iteration, when provenance is inspected, then it traces back to the originating execute-bead session ID and hidden ref — no evaluation-owned provenance record duplicates execute-bead evidence.
393	
394	## Dependencies
395	
396	- FEAT-006 (Agent Service) — harness registry, quorum parallelism, worktree
397	  support, session logging, comparison dispatch
398	- FEAT-014 (Token Awareness) — token/cost tracking per arm
399	- FEAT-012 (Git Awareness) — git worktree operations
400	- DDx Agent library — embedded agent with tool call logging
401	
402	## Out of Scope
403	
404	- **Exec projection** — comparison is a peer to FEAT-010 exec, not a
405	  child. Do not model comparisons as exec runs.
406	- **Exploration loops** — "try 10 ideas to improve metric X" is a workflow
407	  plugin loop that calls DDx comparison primitives, not a DDx execution mode.
408	- **Model selection policies** — workflow tools decide which model for which
409	  task based on comparison results. DDx provides the data.
410	- **Prompt optimization** — automatic prompt rewriting is out of scope.
411	  DDx provides comparison and grading; plugins provide the iteration loop.
412	- **Structured git trailers** — bead + session log already have the data.
413	  Don't duplicate into git commit metadata.
414	- **Autonomy semantics and exploration policy** — DDx does not define what autonomy levels mean or when to run experiments; those are delegated to workflow tools
415	- **Escalation and supervisory rules** — whether to accept a result, retry, or escalate is workflow tool policy; DDx provides the comparison and grading data
416	- Container or VM-level sandboxing
417	- Cross-repo or cross-project comparison
418	
419	## Test Plan
420	
421	See `docs/helix/02-design/solution-designs/SD-023-agent-evaluation.md` and
422	`docs/helix/03-test/test-plans/TP-019-agent-evaluation.md`.
423

## Overview

DDx can dispatch the same prompt to multiple agent harnesses and capture
structured results. This feature adds the evaluation surface: sandboxed
comparison runs that capture side effects, automated grading of outputs,

data.text: Now update the functional requirements to note that CLI dispatch shapes move to skills:

input.old_string:
### Functional

**Sandboxed comparison dispatch**
1. `ddx agent run --compare --harnesses=agent,claude --prompt task.md` runs
   each harness arm in an isolated environment and records a comparison.
2. Each arm runs in a temporary git worktree created from the current HEAD.
   Existing `resolveWorktree` infrastructure is reused.
3. After each arm completes, capture `git diff HEAD` in the worktree as the
   "effect diff" — the concrete artifact of what the agent changed.
4. For DDx Agent arms, also capture the full `ToolCallLog` (every read, write,
   edit, bash call with inputs and outputs).
5. For subprocess arms (codex, claude, opencode), capture stdout/stderr and
   the effect diff. Tool call detail is not available.
6. Temporary worktrees are cleaned up after the comparison unless
   `--keep-sandbox` is specified.
7. Arms run in parallel by default (reuses quorum parallelism). Sequential
   mode available via `--sequential` for resource-constrained environments.

**Side-effect capture**
8. The effect diff is captured as a unified diff string and stored in the
   comparison record alongside the text output.
9. For DDx Agent, the tool call log provides a complete audit trail: which files
   were read, what edits were made, what commands were run, what output they
   produced. This is richer than the diff alone.
10. Optionally run a test suite in each worktree after the agent completes
    (`--post-run "make test"`) and capture pass/fail as a quality signal.
input.new_string:
### Functional

> **Note on workflow shapes:** Comparison dispatch, replay, and benchmark
> execution are implemented as skills (`compare-prompts`, `replay-bead`,
> `benchmark-suite`) in the skills library. FEAT-019 owns the storage schema
> for their outputs and the web UI surfaces that display, aggregate, and manage
> those records. Requirements below describe the evaluation UX layer.

**Comparison record storage and display**
1. Comparison records written by the `compare-prompts` skill (or any skill
   that produces a `ComparisonRecord`) are stored in the FEAT-010 run
   substrate with `type: comparison`.
2. The web UI exposes a comparison detail view: per-arm output, effect diff,
   tool-call summary, test results, and grades — all from the stored record.
3. Comparison list view shows recent comparisons with per-arm summary
   (tokens, cost, duration, pass/fail) sortable and filterable by date,
   harness, and bead.
4. `--format json` output for machine-readable consumption by dun checks
   and CI pipelines.

**Side-effect capture storage**
5. Effect diffs (unified diff strings) and tool call log references are
   stored in the comparison record alongside text output.
6. The UI renders diffs with syntax highlighting; tool call logs are
   available as collapsible detail.

**Grading rubric storage and display**
7. Grading rubrics are stored as named artifacts in the DDx artifact store.
   `ddx agent grade --rubric <file>` loads a rubric by path; named rubrics
   can be stored and referenced by name.
8. The web UI provides a rubric browser: list, view, and edit stored rubrics.
9. Grading results (score, max_score, pass, rationale per arm) are stored in
   the comparison record and displayed in the comparison detail view.
10. Custom grading rubric content is user/workflow-defined. DDx provides
    the storage, retrieval, and display mechanism.

data.text: Now update the Grading primitive section:

input.old_string:
**Grading primitive**
11. `ddx agent grade --comparison <id>` sends a comparison record to a
    grading harness and records the evaluation.
12. `ddx agent grade --comparison <id> --grader claude` specifies which
    harness performs the grading (default: highest-preference available).
13. The grading prompt is a standardized template that presents:
    - The original task prompt
    - Each arm's text output
    - Each arm's effect diff
    - (Optional) test results
14. The grader returns a structured evaluation per arm:
    ```json
    {
      "arm": "agent",
      "score": 7,
      "max_score": 10,
      "pass": true,
      "rationale": "Correct implementation but missed edge case in..."
    }
    ```
15. Grades are appended to the comparison record in the session log.
16. Custom grading rubrics can be provided via `--rubric <file>` to
    replace the default template. DDx provides the rubric loading
    mechanism; the rubric content is user/workflow-defined.

**Comparison reporting**
17. `ddx agent compare --list` shows recent comparison runs with summary.
18. `ddx agent compare --show <id>` displays the full comparison record
    including per-arm outputs, diffs, and grades.
19. `--format json` for machine-readable output (consumed by dun checks,
    workflow evaluation gates, CI pipelines).

**Benchmark suites**
19a. `ddx agent benchmark --suite <path>` loads a JSON suite definition
     with arms (harness/model/tier configurations) and prompts, runs all
     prompts across all arms, and produces aggregate per-arm statistics.
19b. Suite format: `BenchmarkSuite` JSON with `name`, `version`, `arms[]`,
     `prompts[]`, optional `sandbox`, `post_run`, `timeout`.
19c. Output: per-arm summary (completed, failed, total tokens, cost,
     avg duration) plus full comparison records per prompt.
19d. `--output <path>` saves full results as JSON for historical tracking.
input.new_string:
**Grading invocation and display**
11. `ddx agent grade --comparison <id>` sends a comparison record to a
    grading harness and records the evaluation. This is the DDx-owned
    grading invocation surface; dispatch mechanics reuse FEAT-006 harness
    registry.
12. `ddx agent grade --comparison <id> --grader claude` specifies which
    harness performs the grading (default: highest-preference available).
13. The grading prompt is a standardized template that presents:
    - The original task prompt
    - Each arm's text output
    - Each arm's effect diff
    - (Optional) test results
14. The grader returns a structured evaluation per arm stored in the
    comparison record:
    ```json
    {
      "arm": "agent",
      "score": 7,
      "max_score": 10,
      "pass": true,
      "rationale": "Correct implementation but missed edge case in..."
    }
    ```
15. Grades are appended to the comparison record in the FEAT-010 substrate.
16. Custom grading rubrics can be provided via `--rubric <file>` to
    replace the default template. DDx provides the rubric loading and
    storage mechanism; rubric content is user/workflow-defined (see
    requirements 7–10 above).

**Benchmark result aggregation**
17. Benchmark run records (produced by the `benchmark-suite` skill) are
    stored in the FEAT-010 substrate with `type: benchmark`.
18. The web UI provides a benchmark results view: per-arm aggregate
    statistics (completed, failed, total tokens, cost, avg duration) and
    drill-down to individual comparison records per prompt.
19. `--format json` for machine-readable output (consumed by dun checks,
    workflow evaluation gates, CI pipelines).
19a. Historical benchmark results are retained and queryable; the UI
     supports trend views across benchmark runs of the same suite.

input.old_string:
**Replay from bead**
22. `ddx agent replay <bead-id> --model <model> --harness <harness>` is the
    key primitive for answering "what if we reran this bead with a different
    model?"
23. Replay reconstructs the original prompt from the linked session (not from
    bead prose). If the session is unavailable, falls back to bead
    title/description/acceptance as a degraded prompt.
24. Replay base state: by default, replay checks out the `closing_commit_sha`
    parent (the state before the original implementation). `--at-head` mode
    replays against current HEAD instead ("would this model solve the task
    today?"). The default answers "would this model have produced the same
    result then?"
    For tracker-only backfills, do not point `closing_commit_sha` at a later
    metadata-only close commit; omit the field so replay falls back to the
    sessionless/manual baseline instead of pretending the metadata commit was
    the governed implementation diff.
25. Replay runs in a sandbox worktree, captures diff, runs `--post-run`
    evaluation, and reports a comparison of the new diff against the
    original commit's diff.
input.new_string:
**Replay result display**
22. Replay execution is handled by the `replay-bead` skill. FEAT-019 owns
    the storage schema for replay records and the web UI display surface.
23. Replay records are stored in the FEAT-010 substrate with `type: replay`,
    linked to the originating bead via `bead_id` and `session_id`.
24. The web UI shows a replay detail view: the new diff, the baseline diff,
    a side-by-side comparison, and any `--post-run` test result.
25. The bead detail view in the web UI includes a "Replays" tab listing all
    replay records for that bead with model/harness/outcome summary.

data.text: Now update the Design Principles section to reflect the new scope:

input.old_string:
### Platform primitive, not framework

DDx provides the mechanics:
- Run N harnesses on the same prompt in sandboxes
- Capture outputs and side effects
- Send results to a grading harness
- Record everything

DDx does **not** provide:
- Prompt optimization strategies
- Benchmark suites or leaderboards
- Model selection policies
- Grading rubric content (beyond a sensible default)

Workflow tools compose these primitives. For example, a workflow plugin might
define: "Before promoting a bead, run the implementation prompt through
agent+claude, require grade ≥8/10 on both arms." A check runner might define:
"regression-test this prompt against the recorded baseline."
input.new_string:
### Evaluation UX, not execution framework

FEAT-019 provides the storage schema and UI surfaces for evaluation results:
- Store and display comparison records (written by skills)
- Store, manage, and display grading rubrics
- Aggregate and display benchmark results
- Invoke the grading harness and record results

FEAT-019 does **not** provide:
- Comparison dispatch (→ `compare-prompts` skill)
- Replay execution (→ `replay-bead` skill)
- Benchmark execution (→ `benchmark-suite` skill)
- Prompt optimization strategies
- Model selection policies
- Grading rubric content (beyond a sensible default)

Skills compose DDx primitives into workflow shapes. FEAT-019 stores and
surfaces what skills produce.

### Sandboxing strategy

Git worktrees are the natural sandbox for code-generating agents:
- Fast to create (copy-on-write on modern filesystems)
- Full git state (HEAD, index, config, hooks)
- Clean isolation (separate working directory)
- Cheap to clean up (`git worktree remove`)
- Diff capture is trivial (`git diff HEAD`)

For DDx Agent specifically, the tool sandbox is already built into the
`WorkDir` parameter — setting it to the worktree path is sufficient.
For subprocess harnesses, `WorkDirFlag` (codex: `-C`, opencode: `--dir`)
achieves the same.

No container or VM sandboxing is needed for comparison runs because:
- The threat model is "prevent cross-arm interference," not "prevent
  malicious code execution"
- Worktrees provide filesystem isolation
- Network isolation is out of scope (agents need API access)

If stronger isolation is needed (e.g., untrusted model output running
shell commands), that's a future concern addressed by DDx Agent's tool
permission layer or external sandbox tooling.

292	**As a** CI system ensuring prompt quality

295

304	**As a** developer running comparison experiments

306	**So that** file changes from one arm don't contaminate the other or my
307	working tree
308

311	  each arm's changes are captured as separate diffs
312	- Given the comparison completes, then my original working tree is
313	  unchanged
314	- Given an arm fails mid-run, then its worktree is still cleaned up
315	  (unless `--keep-sandbox`)
316	
317	### US-194: Developer Replays a Bead With a Different Model
318	**As a** developer evaluating local model capability
319	**I want** to rerun a previously-closed bead with a different model
320	**So that** I can compare the new model's output against the known-good result
321

324	  `ddx agent replay <bead-id> --model qwen3.5-27b --harness agent`,

326	- Given the replay completes in a sandbox worktree checked out at the

328	  the original commit's diff

331	  prose and reports "baseline session unknown"

12	# Feature: DDx Agent Evaluation UX
13	
14	**ID:** FEAT-019
15	**Status:** In Progress
16	**Priority:** P1
17	**Owner:** DDx Team
18	
19	## Overview
20	
21	FEAT-019 owns the **evaluation UX layer**: comparison views, grading rubric
22	storage and display, and benchmark result aggregation in the web UI. It is a
23	child of FEAT-010 (exec substrate) — not a peer.
24	
25	Workflow shapes (comparison dispatch, replay, benchmark execution) live in the
26	skills library (`compare-prompts`, `replay-bead`, `benchmark-suite`). FEAT-019
27	provides the storage, display, and aggregation surfaces that those skills write
28	into and that users read from.
29	
30	This is deliberately an **evaluation and reporting layer** built on top of the
31	FEAT-010 run substrate and FEAT-006 agent service. The primary bead-execution
32	workflow is `ddx try` (FEAT-010), which runs an agent, captures evidence, and
33	lands or preserves the iteration. FEAT-019 adds: grading rubric management,
34	comparison record display, and benchmark result aggregation — it does not define
35	a competing foundational execution model or dispatch mechanism.
36	
37	### Ownership split
38	
39	- **FEAT-006** owns `execute-bead` (the canonical bead execution workflow),
40	  comparison dispatch (`ddx agent run --compare`), `ComparisonRecord`
41	  persistence, and arm execution mechanics.
42	- **FEAT-019** (this spec) owns **grading**, **replay**, and
43	  **benchmark suite execution** — the evaluation and reporting layer built
44	  on top of execute-bead preserved iterations and comparison dispatch.
45	- **FEAT-010** owns the exec substrate. FEAT-019 is a child of FEAT-010:
46	  comparison records, grading results, and benchmark aggregations are stored
47	  in the FEAT-010 run substrate. Workflow dispatch shapes (comparison,
48	  replay, benchmark) live in the skills library, not in DDx core.
49	- **FEAT-004** owns bead semantics including evidence. FEAT-019 adds
50	  evidence fields (session linkage) but does not modify bead close
51	  behavior.
52	
53	### Relationship to execute-bead
54	
55	`ddx agent execute-bead` is the primary primitive for agent-driven bead
56	execution. FEAT-019 replay and benchmark capabilities are built on preserved
57	`execute-bead` iterations: multiple `execute-bead --no-merge` attempts from
58	the same base produce a corpus that FEAT-019 can compare, grade, and report
59	on. "Try N ideas on this bead and pick the best" is a workflow plugin loop
60	that calls `execute-bead --no-merge` repeatedly and then uses FEAT-019
61	evaluation primitives — it is not a DDx execution mode.
62	
63	### Relationship to workflow tools
64	
65	DDx owns the comparison, grading, and replay primitives. Workflow plugins own
66	the policies that use them: when to experiment, which variables to sweep,
67	quality gates using experiment results, automatic model selection, and
68	exploration loops. "Try 10 ideas to improve metric X" is a workflow plugin loop
69	that calls DDx comparison primitives — it is not a DDx execution mode.
70	
71	## Problem Statement
72	
73	**Current situation:**
74	- `ddx agent run` can dispatch to any harness and capture output/tokens/cost.
75	- Quorum mode runs multiple harnesses and checks consensus (pass/fail).
76	- DDx Agent gives DDx full control of the agent loop with tool call logging.
77	- Beads define work. Commits capture verified results. But there is no
78	  structured way to compare harness outputs for the same prompt, replay a
79	  bead with a different model, or grade the quality of results.
80	
81	**Pain points:**
82	- When evaluating whether a local model (via DDx Agent) can replace a cloud
83	  model for a task class, there's no structured way to compare.
84	- Side-effecting agent runs (file writes, shell commands) can't be safely
85	  compared without isolation — running two agents in the same worktree
86	  produces interference.
87	- No way to answer "is this prompt good enough?" without manual review.
88	- Closed beads don't record which model/harness produced the result, so
89	  replaying with a different model requires manual prompt reconstruction.
90	
91	**Desired outcome:** Comparison, grading, and replay primitives that let
92	operators answer: "For this prompt, how does harness A compare to harness
93	B?" and "What if we reran this bead with a different model?" — with
94	concrete artifacts (diffs, outputs, grades) — automatically and repeatably.
95	
96	The canonical architecture is
97	[`SD-023`](../../02-design/solution-designs/SD-023-agent-evaluation.md).
98	
99	## Requirements
100	
101	### Functional
102	
103	> **Note on workflow shapes:** Comparison dispatch, replay, and benchmark
104	> execution are implemented as skills (`compare-prompts`, `replay-bead`,
105	> `benchmark-suite`) in the skills library. FEAT-019 owns the storage schema
106	> for their outputs and the web UI surfaces that display, aggregate, and manage
107	> those records. Requirements below describe the evaluation UX layer.
108	
109	**Comparison record storage and display**
110	1. Comparison records written by the `compare-prompts` skill (or any skill
111	   that produces a `ComparisonRecord`) are stored in the FEAT-010 run
112	   substrate with `type: comparison`.
113	2. The web UI exposes a comparison detail view: per-arm output, effect diff,
114	   tool-call summary, test results, and grades — all from the stored record.
115	3. Comparison list view shows recent comparisons with per-arm summary
116	   (tokens, cost, duration, pass/fail) sortable and filterable by date,
117	   harness, and bead.
118	4. `--format json` output for machine-readable consumption by dun checks
119	   and CI pipelines.
120	
121	**Side-effect capture storage**
122	5. Effect diffs (unified diff strings) and tool call log references are
123	   stored in the comparison record alongside text output.
124	6. The UI renders diffs with syntax highlighting; tool call logs are
125	   available as collapsible detail.
126	
127	**Grading rubric storage and display**
128	7. Grading rubrics are stored as named artifacts in the DDx artifact store.
129	   `ddx agent grade --rubric <file>` loads a rubric by path; named rubrics
130	   can be stored and referenced by name.
131	8. The web UI provides a rubric browser: list, view, and edit stored rubrics.
132	9. Grading results (score, max_score, pass, rationale per arm) are stored in
133	   the comparison record and displayed in the comparison detail view.
134	10. Custom grading rubric content is user/workflow-defined. DDx provides
135	    the storage, retrieval, and display mechanism.
136	
137	**Grading invocation and display**
138	11. `ddx agent grade --comparison <id>` sends a comparison record to a
139	    grading harness and records the evaluation. This is the DDx-owned
140	    grading invocation surface; dispatch mechanics reuse FEAT-006 harness
141	    registry.
142	12. `ddx agent grade --comparison <id> --grader claude` specifies which
143	    harness performs the grading (default: highest-preference available).
144	13. The grading prompt is a standardized template that presents:
145	    - The original task prompt
146	    - Each arm's text output
147	    - Each arm's effect diff
148	    - (Optional) test results
149	14. The grader returns a structured evaluation per arm stored in the
150	    comparison record:
151	    ```json
152	    {
153	      "arm": "agent",
154	      "score": 7,
155	      "max_score": 10,
156	      "pass": true,
157	      "rationale": "Correct implementation but missed edge case in..."
158	    }
159	    ```
160	15. Grades are appended to the comparison record in the FEAT-010 substrate.
161	16. Custom grading rubrics can be provided via `--rubric <file>` to
162	    replace the default template. DDx provides the rubric loading and
163	    storage mechanism; rubric content is user/workflow-defined (see
164	    requirements 7–10 above).
165	
166	**Benchmark result aggregation**
167	17. Benchmark run records (produced by the `benchmark-suite` skill) are
168	    stored in the FEAT-010 substrate with `type: benchmark`.
169	18. The web UI provides a benchmark results view: per-arm aggregate
170	    statistics (completed, failed, total tokens, cost, avg duration) and
171	    drill-down to individual comparison records per prompt.
172	19. `--format json` for machine-readable output (consumed by dun checks,
173	    workflow evaluation gates, CI pipelines).
174	19a. Historical benchmark results are retained and queryable; the UI
175	     supports trend views across benchmark runs of the same suite.
176	
177	**Bead-session linkage** (foundation for replay)
178	20. When a bead is closed after an agent run, record the `session_id` (from
179	    `sessions.jsonl`) and `closing_commit_sha` as bead evidence. The session
180	    already owns model, harness, tokens, and cost — do not duplicate those
181	    fields on the bead. `ddx bead show` resolves agent metadata from the
182	    linked session.
183	21. If no session link is recorded (manual close, legacy beads), replay
184	    still works but reports "baseline session unknown" and cannot provide
185	    a diff comparison.
186	
187	**Replay result display**
188	22. Replay execution is handled by the `replay-bead` skill. FEAT-019 owns
189	    the storage schema for replay records and the web UI display surface.
190	23. Replay records are stored in the FEAT-010 substrate with `type: replay`,
191	    linked to the originating bead via `bead_id` and `session_id`.
192	24. The web UI shows a replay detail view: the new diff, the baseline diff,
193	    a side-by-side comparison, and any `--post-run` test result.
194	25. The bead detail view in the web UI includes a "Replays" tab listing all
195	    replay records for that bead with model/harness/outcome summary.
196	
197	### Non-Functional
198	
199	- **Isolation:** Worktree sandboxes must prevent cross-arm interference.
200	  Each arm sees a clean copy of HEAD.
201	- **Performance:** Parallel arm execution. Worktree creation is fast
202	  (git worktree add is <1s for typical repos).
203	- **Storage:** Comparison records include diffs and outputs which can be
204	  large. Use the same attachment-backed storage as session logs (FEAT-006).
205	- **Determinism:** For DDx Agent with the virtual provider, comparison runs
206	  are fully deterministic — enables CI-based prompt regression testing.
207	
208	## Design Principles
209	
210	### Evaluation UX, not execution framework
211	
212	FEAT-019 provides the storage schema and UI surfaces for evaluation results:
213	- Store and display comparison records (written by skills)
214	- Store, manage, and display grading rubrics
215	- Aggregate and display benchmark results
216	- Invoke the grading harness and record results
217	
218	FEAT-019 does **not** provide:
219	- Comparison dispatch (→ `compare-prompts` skill)
220	- Replay execution (→ `replay-bead` skill)
221	- Benchmark execution (→ `benchmark-suite` skill)
222	- Prompt optimization strategies
223	- Model selection policies
224	- Grading rubric content (beyond a sensible default)
225	
226	Skills compose DDx primitives into workflow shapes. FEAT-019 stores and
227	surfaces what skills produce.
228	
229	## CLI Interface
230	
231	> **Workflow dispatch shapes** (`compare`, `replay`, `benchmark`) are skills
232	> in the skills library. FEAT-019 owns the grading invocation surface and the
233	> record query/display surface.
234	
235	```bash
236	# Grade a comparison using claude as grader
237	ddx agent grade --comparison cmp-abc123 --grader claude
238	
239	# Grade with custom rubric
240	ddx agent grade --comparison cmp-abc123 --rubric rubrics/code-quality.md
241	
242	# List recent comparisons
243	ddx agent compare --list
244	
245	# Show comparison detail
246	ddx agent compare --show cmp-abc123 --format json
247	
248	# Workflow dispatch shapes are skills — not DDx CLI core:
249	# compare-prompts skill:  N-arm dispatch + aggregation
250	# replay-bead skill:      re-run with altered conditions + baseline diff
251	# benchmark-suite skill:  compare across prompt matrix
252	```
253	
254	### Configuration
255	
256	```yaml
257	# .ddx/config.yaml
258	agent:
259	  compare:
260	    default_grader: claude         # harness to use for grading
261	```
262	
263	## User Stories
264	
265	### US-190: Developer Views Comparison Results in Web UI
266	**As a** developer evaluating whether a local model can handle a task class
267	**I want** to view comparison records in the web UI after the `compare-prompts` skill runs
268	**So that** I can see concrete differences in output quality and cost
269	
270	**Acceptance Criteria:**
271	- Given a comparison record exists in the FEAT-010 substrate, then the web UI
272	  comparison list shows per-arm summary (tokens, cost, duration)
273	- Given I open a comparison detail view, then I see per-arm output, effect diff,
274	  and grades (if graded)
275	- Given `--format json`, then machine-readable comparison output is available
276	  via CLI for CI consumption
277	
278	### US-191: Developer Grades Agent Outputs
279	**As a** developer assessing prompt quality
280	**I want** to have a smart model grade the outputs of a comparison run
281	**So that** I get a structured quality score without manual review
282	
283	**Acceptance Criteria:**
284	- Given a completed comparison, when I run `ddx agent grade --comparison <id>`,
285	  then a grading harness evaluates each arm
286	- Given the grading completes, then each arm has a score, pass/fail, and
287	  rationale in the comparison record
288	- Given I specify `--rubric custom.md`, then the custom rubric replaces
289	  the default grading template
290	
291	### US-192: CI Pipeline Runs Prompt Regression Test
292	**As a** CI system ensuring prompt quality
293	**I want** to compare agent outputs against a recorded baseline
294	**So that** prompt changes that degrade quality are caught before merge
295	
296	**Acceptance Criteria:**
297	- Given a comparison with `--harnesses=agent` and the virtual provider
298	  baseline, when the DDx Agent output diverges significantly, then the
299	  comparison record reflects the difference
300	- Given `--format json` output, then CI can parse the comparison and
301	  fail the pipeline on grade regression
302	
303	### US-193: Developer Inspects Effect Diffs in Comparison View
304	**As a** developer reviewing comparison experiment results
305	**I want** to see each arm's effect diff in the comparison detail view
306	**So that** I can understand what each model actually changed in the codebase
307	
308	**Acceptance Criteria:**
309	- Given a comparison record with effect diffs, then the web UI comparison
310	  detail view renders each arm's diff with syntax highlighting
311	- Given a comparison has tool call logs, then they are accessible as
312	  collapsible detail in the UI
313	- Given an arm failed, then its failure reason is shown in the comparison view
314	
315	### US-194: Developer Views Replay Results on Bead Detail
316	**As a** developer evaluating local model capability
317	**I want** to view replay results in the bead detail view after the `replay-bead` skill runs
318	**So that** I can compare the new model's output against the known-good result
319	
320	**Acceptance Criteria:**
321	- Given a replay record exists for a bead, then the bead detail view includes
322	  a "Replays" tab listing all replays with model/harness/outcome summary
323	- Given I open a replay detail view, then I see the new diff, the baseline diff,
324	  and a side-by-side comparison
325	- Given a replay has a `--post-run` test result, then pass/fail is shown in the
326	  replay detail view
327	
328	### US-195: Bead Links to Agent Session on Close
329	**As a** developer building a replay corpus
330	**I want** bead close to record the session ID and closing commit
331	**So that** future replays can reconstruct the exact prompt and baseline
332	
333	**Acceptance Criteria:**
334	- Given an agent run closes bead ddx-xxx, then `session_id` and
335	  `closing_commit_sha` are recorded as bead evidence
336	- Given `ddx bead show ddx-xxx`, then model/harness/tokens/cost are
337	  resolved from the linked session (not stored on the bead)
338	- Given a bead closed without an agent run, then no session link exists
339	  and `ddx bead show` omits agent metadata
340	
341	### US-196: Evaluation UI Displays Records from Preserved Try Iterations
342	**As** a developer evaluating bead execution quality
343	**I want** FEAT-019 UI to display comparison/grading records sourced from preserved `ddx try` iterations
344	**So that** the evaluation UX surfaces what skills produce from preserved iterations — not a parallel execution model
345	
346	**Acceptance Criteria:**
347	- Given preserved `ddx try` iterations exist for a bead, when skills write
348	  comparison or benchmark records referencing those iterations, then FEAT-019
349	  UI displays them without requiring a separate evaluation execution path.
350	- Given an evaluation record references a preserved try iteration, when
351	  provenance is inspected in the UI, then it traces back to the originating
352	  try session ID and hidden ref — no FEAT-019-owned duplicate provenance record exists.
353	
354	## Dependencies
355	
356	- FEAT-010 (Executions) — run substrate; comparison/replay/benchmark records
357	  are stored here; FEAT-019 is a child of FEAT-010
358	- FEAT-006 (Agent Service) — harness registry, session logging, grading dispatch
359	- FEAT-008 (Web UI) — UI rendering surfaces for comparison/replay/benchmark views
360	- FEAT-014 (Token Awareness) — token/cost tracking per arm (displayed in comparison views)
361	- FEAT-012 (Git Awareness) — git diff rendering in comparison views
362	- Skills library — `compare-prompts`, `replay-bead`, `benchmark-suite` write the
363	  records that FEAT-019 displays
364	
365	## Out of Scope
366	
367	- **Comparison dispatch** — running N harnesses on the same prompt is the
368	  `compare-prompts` skill, not DDx core.
369	- **Replay execution** — running a bead with a different model is the
370	  `replay-bead` skill, not DDx core.
371	- **Benchmark execution** — running a prompt matrix is the `benchmark-suite`
372	  skill, not DDx core.
373	- **Exploration loops** — "try 10 ideas to improve metric X" is a workflow
374	  plugin loop that calls skills, not a DDx execution mode.
375	- **Model selection policies** — workflow tools decide which model for which
376	  task based on comparison results. DDx provides the data.
377	- **Prompt optimization** — automatic prompt rewriting is out of scope.
378	  DDx provides comparison and grading; plugins provide the iteration loop.
379	- **Structured git trailers** — bead + session log already have the data.
380	  Don't duplicate into git commit metadata.
381	- **Autonomy semantics and exploration policy** — DDx does not define what autonomy levels mean or when to run experiments; those are delegated to workflow tools
382	- **Escalation and supervisory rules** — whether to accept a result, retry, or escalate is workflow tool policy; DDx provides the comparison and grading data
383	- Container or VM-level sandboxing
384	- Cross-repo or cross-project comparison
385	
386	## Test Plan
387	
388	See `docs/helix/02-design/solution-designs/SD-023-agent-evaluation.md` and
389	`docs/helix/03-test/test-plans/TP-019-agent-evaluation.md`.
390

input.old_string:
### Relationship to execute-bead

`ddx agent execute-bead` is the primary primitive for agent-driven bead
execution. FEAT-019 replay and benchmark capabilities are built on preserved
`execute-bead` iterations: multiple `execute-bead --no-merge` attempts from
the same base produce a corpus that FEAT-019 can compare, grade, and report
on. "Try N ideas on this bead and pick the best" is a workflow plugin loop
that calls `execute-bead --no-merge` repeatedly and then uses FEAT-019
evaluation primitives — it is not a DDx execution mode.

### Relationship to workflow tools

DDx owns the comparison, grading, and replay primitives. Workflow plugins own
the policies that use them: when to experiment, which variables to sweep,
quality gates using experiment results, automatic model selection, and
exploration loops. "Try 10 ideas to improve metric X" is a workflow plugin loop
that calls DDx comparison primitives — it is not a DDx execution mode.
input.new_string:
### Relationship to the run substrate (FEAT-010)

FEAT-019 is a child of FEAT-010. Comparison, replay, and benchmark records
are stored in the FEAT-010 run substrate. FEAT-019 defines the storage schema
extensions (`type: comparison`, `type: replay`, `type: benchmark`) and owns
the web UI surfaces that display those records. It does not own worktree
management, run lifecycle, or dispatch mechanics.

### Relationship to workflow skills and tools

Workflow shapes live in the skills library:
- `compare-prompts` — N-arm dispatch + aggregation
- `replay-bead` — re-run with altered conditions + baseline diff
- `benchmark-suite` — compare across prompt matrix

DDx owns grading invocation (`ddx agent grade`) and the evaluation UX.
Workflow plugins own the policies: when to experiment, which variables to
sweep, quality gates using grading results, automatic model selection, and
exploration loops.
